topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom air hummed with forced gaiety, a symphony of polite laughter and clinking glasses. I kept my gaze fixed on the floral arrangements, a deliberate wall between me and the inevitable. Then, across the room, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. Standing near the buffet, looking impossibly polished, radiating a kind of effortless charm that used to feel like a physical blow. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I took a slow sip of my champagne, the bubbles doing little to settle the knot in my stomach. I needed distance, a clear path to the exit, but every turn of my body felt like an act of defiance against my own rising tide of discomfort. He looked up, and for a split second, the practiced mask slipped, revealing a flicker of something—recognition, maybe regret—before he smoothly turned back to his conversation. I didn't move. I just stood there, rooted to the spot, letting the silence between us stretch taut.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," Sarah chirped, nudging me forward. I managed a tight smile that felt brittle on my face. I watched the entrance, and there he was. Liam. He was laughing, his arm draped casually around someone else’s shoulder, looking every bit the picture of someone who had moved on seamlessly. A strange heat rose in my chest, a stubborn refusal to let this moment derail my carefully constructed composure. I gripped my glass so hard my knuckles turned white. I wanted to turn away, to melt into the wallpaper, but my feet seemed glued to the marble floor. When his eyes swept over mine—a brief, charged connection—I held the contact just a fraction too long, a silent challenge in my steady stare. I didn't break eye contact. I simply waited for him to acknowledge the tension, to break the spell, and then I could finally breathe again.

[story 3]

I found myself cornered near the terrace doors, pretending intense interest in the distant city lights. That’s when I saw him. He was talking animatedly with Marcus, his posture relaxed, the very picture of contentment. A sudden, sharp clench tightened my core. I felt a distinct urge to walk directly toward him, to confront the awkward proximity, but some internal mechanism held me back, locking my muscles in place. It felt like fighting a current. I deliberately focused on the pattern of the stone railing beneath my fingers, counting the imperfections in the mortar. When he finally glanced in my direction, his expression shifted—a momentary pause, a flicker of something complicated crossing his features. I met his gaze, holding it steady, refusing to yield any ground. I didn't offer a smile, didn't flinch. I just continued to observe, a statue carved from sheer refusal to react.
